Document [1] (Title: Schizophrenia -- Etiology) Twin and family studies indicate that genetic factors can explain approximately 80% of the risk for schizophrenia. [1] While linked common genetic variants have less impact on risk, rare mutations have a more significant impact. [10] One such rare mutation is the chromosome 22q11.2 deletion, which increases lifetime risk 25-fold. [4] Genome-wide association studies have identified 130 genes predominantly associated with neural differentiation, organization, and transmission, which increase the risk of schizophrenia. [11] Most of these genes are not found in coding regions but exert their influence through gene expression modulation. [11] Notably, nearly 30% of these genes are involved in the functioning of the presynaptic and postsynaptic elements of the glutamatergic synapse by impacting N -methyl-D-aspartic acid (NMDA) receptor transmission. [11] Thus, schizophrenia is a polygenic disorder resulting from the combined effects of multiple genes spread across the genome, each contributing modestly.

Document [10] (Title: Schizophrenia -- Etiology) The neurodevelopmental hypothesis of schizophrenia proposes that a mix of genetic risks and environmental factors during early brain development leads to the disorder. These influences, particularly significant in the prenatal and early life stages, set the stage for the emergence of symptoms of schizophrenia in early adulthood. [13]
Document [11] (Title: Psychosis -- History and Physical) Disorganized thought usually gets illuminated through the patient’s speech and general communication patterns. In a mental status examination, one should be able to say a person not suffering from a psychotic disorder exhibits a “logical” and “goal-directed” thought process. In psychosis, one can see loosened associations or sequences of unrelated or loosely related ideas. Circumstantial thought, or a “non-linear thought pattern,” is where a person is unable to give a direct answer to a question without excessive or unnecessary detail. Tangential thought is where a person continually drifts from the topic of conversation, never to return to the original point. Word salad is an unintelligible or incoherent jumble of words. Neologisms are made up of words or phrases. Perseveration is the repetition of words and statements.

Document [15] (Title: Genetics, Nondisjunction -- Clinical Significance -- Autosomal Trisomies) Patau syndrome: Trisomy of chromosome 13 Clinical Features: Rocker-bottom feet, microphthalmia (abnormally small eyes), microcephaly (abnormally small head), polydactyly, holoprosencephaly, cleft lip and palate, congenital heart disease, and severe intellectual disability. Life expectancy is seldom longer than one year. Edwards syndrome: Trisomy of chromosome 18 Clinical Features: Rocker-bottom feet, low set ears, micrognathia (abnormally small jaw), clenched hands with overlapping fingers, congenital heart disease, and severe intellectual disability. Life expectancy is normally less than one year. Down syndrome: Trisomy of chromosome 21 The most common viable aneuploidy. Clinical Features: Single palmar crease, flat facies, prominent epicanthal folds, duodenal atresia, congenital heart disease, Hirschsprung disease, intellectual disability. Notably increased risk to develop Alzheimer's disease or leukemia. Life expectancy is about 60 years.

Document [19] (Title: Study Bias -- Issues of Concern -- Planning) Two other important forms of selection bias are lead-time bias and length time bias. Lead-time bias occurs in the context of disease diagnosis. In general, it occurs when new diagnostic testing allows detection of a disease in an early stage, causing a false appearance of longer lifespan or improved outcomes. [14] An example of this is noted in individuals with schizophrenia with varying durations of untreated psychosis. Those with shorter durations of psychosis relative to longer durations typically had better psychosocial functioning after admission to and treatment within a hospital. However, upon further analysis, it was found that it was not the duration of psychosis that affected psychosocial functioning. Rather, the duration of psychosis was indicative of the stage of the person’s disease, and those individuals with shorter durations of psychosis were in an earlier stage of their disease. [15]

They do not show any developmental regression, and they might become better with some self-help skills with supportive care. Document [8] (Title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 1234) Senility and organic mental disorders is a broad diagnostic category that encompasses several related conditions. It includes a child entity described as “dementie; seniel, met acute verwardheidstoestand,” indicating a form of dementia in the elderly that may present with an acute confused or delirious state. Within this overarching category, Senility and organic mental disorders classifies schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders, linking late‑life psychosis to the broader spectrum of organic mental illness. It is also classified as “خرف وعائي مصحوب حالة مزاجية اكتئابية,” which refers to vascular dementia accompanied by a depressive mood, highlighting the frequent coexistence of cerebrovascular pathology, cognitive decline, and mood disturbance in older adults. Another classification, “ذُهان شيخوخي,” denotes senile psychosis, emphasizing that psychotic features can arise as a direct manifestation of age‑related brain changes. Additionally, Senility and organic mental disorders is classified as “ضمور المخ المحدد,” a term for specific brain atrophy, underscoring the role of structural neurodegeneration in the development of these conditions. The emotional blunting (“تبلد المشاعر”) observed in affected patients is identified as a manifestation of this specific brain atrophy, illustrating how neuroanatomical deterioration can lead to reduced affective responsiveness. Together, these interrelated concepts portray senility‑related organic mental disorders as a spectrum that includes dementia with acute confusion, vascular dementia with depression, late‑life psychosis, and distinct patterns of brain atrophy, all of which contribute to the clinical presentation and guide comprehensive patient care.

Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2595) Benperidol, chlorpromazine, levomepromazine maleate, prochlorperazine mesylate, and trifluoperazine are all pharmacologic agents that may be used to treat اضطراب ذُهاني (psychotic disorder). These drugs belong to the class of typical antipsychotics and exert their therapeutic effect primarily through antagonism of dopamine D₂ receptors, thereby reducing the positive symptoms of psychosis such as delusions and hallucinations. Chlorpromazine, the first widely used antipsychotic, and its related compounds levomepromazine maleate and prochlorperazine mesylate share similar mechanisms and are employed in both acute and chronic management of psychotic states. Benperidol and trifluoperazine, also dopamine antagonists, are utilized when additional potency or specific side‑effect profiles are desired. The inclusion of these agents in treatment regimens underscores the importance of selecting an appropriate antipsychotic based on efficacy, tolerability, and patient‑specific factors to achieve optimal control of psychotic symptoms and improve overall patient care.
